answers.intake_q1: When did your back pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly your back pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or trauma to your back or ribs?
answers.intake_q4: Any fever, chills, or unexplained weight loss since the pain started?
answers.intake_q5: Have you noticed any shortness of breath, coughing, or chest pain along with your back pain?
answers.intake_q6: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your back pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a6: I tried tylenol but it didn't really help much
answers.intake_q7: Any numbness, weakness, or tingling in your legs or feet?
answers.intake_q8: Any history of kidney problems, urinary symptoms, or recent infections?
answers.intake_q9: Have you noticed any swelling, bruising, or tenderness over the area where your back pain is located?
answers.intake_q10: Any history of osteoporosis, cancer, or recent travel?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with left-sided lower rib cage back pain that started approximately three weeks ago. She denies any recent injury or trauma, fever, chills, unexplained weight loss, shortness of breath, coughing, or chest pain. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) with minimal relief. She reports tenderness over the affected area but no numbness, weakness, tingling in the legs or feet, or urinary symptoms. There is no history of osteoporosis, cancer, or recent travel. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: 
The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal back pain localized to the left lower rib cage, likely due to soft tissue strain or inflammation given the tenderness and absence of systemic symptoms. 
Differential: 
1. Musculoskeletal strain or soft tissue injury 
2. Costochondritis or rib inflammation 
3. Herpes zoster (early stage without rash) 
4. Referred pain from visceral causes (e.g., renal or gastrointestinal) 

P: 
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid NSAIDs if there is a history of renal disease, liver disease, or gastrointestinal bleeding; use acetaminophen cautiously in liver disease.
 - Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
 - Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen for pain and inflammation relief. (avoid in renal disease; especially severe CKD/dialysis)
 - Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek immediate care if she develops fever, worsening pain, neurological symptoms (numbness, weakness), chest pain, shortness of breath, or signs of infection such as rash or swelling. 
 - Testing: No immediate testing indicated unless symptoms worsen or new concerning features develop.

evidence.title: Back Pain Symptoms, Types, & Causes | NIAMS
evidence.snippet: Back pain is a common medical problem. Many factors may cause different types of back pain. Learn the parts of the back & what may be causing your back pain.

evidence.title: Pain Management Medications - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
evidence.snippet: Analgesics are medications used in the management and treatment of pain. They include several classes of medications (acetaminophen, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, antidepressants, antiepileptics, local anesthetics, and opioids). Thi

evidence.title: Medicines for back pain: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
evidence.snippet: Acute back pain often goes away on its own over several weeks. In some people, back pain persists. It may not go away completely or it may get more painful at times.
